additionalTime: 30 minutes to 2 hours buttermilk soak plus 15 minutes coating rest and 3 minutes final rest
author: Robert Carr
description: Chicken fried steak with evenly tenderized top round or cube steak, a seasoned hot sauce buttermilk soak, a rough two-layer flour coating, carefully managed shallow fry, and peppered cream gravy built from clean toasted pan drippings.
equipment: Cast-iron skillet or heavy saute pan
Oven, optional for holding steak warm

groceryIngredients.item: tenderized top round steak or cube steak, ideally 1/3 inch thick and no thicker than 1/2 inch
neutral frying oil, preferably peanut oil

ingredients: 1 tenderized top round steak or cube steak, about 6 to 8 ounces and about 1/3 inch thick, up to 1/2 inch max
1 teaspoon Louisiana-style cayenne hot sauce, such as Frank's RedHot, Crystal, or Texas Pete
1 1/2 teaspoons Diamond Crystal kosher salt or 1 teaspoon Morton kosher salt, divided
1 1/4 teaspoons black pepper, freshly ground if possible, divided
Small pinch ground white pepper, for the gravy
Small pinch celery salt, for the gravy
2 cups neutral frying oil, preferably peanut oil, or enough for 1/2 inch depth in a small skillet
1 tablespoon finely chopped fresh parsley, optional, for finishing
instructions: Set 1 wire rack inside a rimmed baking sheet for the breaded raw steak, then set a second clean wire rack inside another rimmed baking sheet for the fried steak.
Pat the tenderized top round steak or cube steak very dry with paper towels.

If starting with plain top round instead of already-tenderized cube steak, cover the steak with plastic wrap or parchment and pound it with the textured side of a meat mallet until it is evenly tenderized and about 1/3 inch thick, but no thicker than 1/2 inch.
If an already-tenderized steak is thicker than 1/2 inch, cover it with plastic wrap or parchment and gently pound it to an even 1/3 to 1/2 inch thickness with a meat mallet or rolling pin.
Season both sides of the steak with 1/4 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt and 1/8 teaspoon black pepper.
Let the seasoned steak sit for 10 minutes while you make the buttermilk mixture so the salt starts dissolving into the surface instead of staying dry on top.
In a shallow bowl, whisk together 1/2 cup buttermilk, 1 large egg, 1 teaspoon Louisiana-style cayenne hot sauce, and 1/4 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt.
Add the seasoned steak to the buttermilk mixture and turn until fully coated.
Cover and refrigerate the steak in the buttermilk mixture for at least 30 minutes, or up to 2 hours for deeper seasoning without making the coating or meat taste overly tangy.
About 20 minutes before breading, remove the bowl of steak from the refrigerator so the steak is not ice-cold when it hits the oil.
Before breading, measure 2 tablespoons all-purpose flour into a clean small bowl and reserve it for the gravy so it never touches raw steak.

Make the seasoned flour coating in a shallow bowl by whisking together the remaining 1/2 cup plus 2 tablespoons all-purpose flour, 2 tablespoons cornstarch, 1/4 teaspoon baking powder, 1/2 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt, 1/2 teaspoon black pepper, 1/2 teaspoon smoked paprika, 1/4 teaspoon garlic powder, 1/4 teaspoon onion powder, 1/8 teaspoon cayenne pepper, 1/8 teaspoon mustard powder, and 1/8 teaspoon MSG if using.

Drizzle 1 tablespoon of the buttermilk mixture into the seasoned flour and rub it through the flour with your fingers until the flour has pea-size clumps. Those clumps become the extra-crunchy ridges.
Remove 1 steak from the buttermilk mixture and let the excess drip back into the bowl for 5 seconds.
Press the steak firmly into the seasoned flour on both sides, making sure flour gets into every tenderized groove.
Dip the floured steak back into the buttermilk mixture for 1 to 2 seconds, then let the excess drip off again.
Press the steak into the seasoned flour a second time, packing the clumpy flour onto both sides until there are no damp bare spots.
Shake off only loose dusty flour, then place the breaded steak on the raw-steak wire rack.
Let the breaded steak rest on the raw-steak wire rack for 15 minutes so the flour coating absorbs moisture and sticks during frying.
While the coating rests, add 2 cups neutral frying oil to a small cast-iron skillet or heavy saute pan, or use enough oil to reach 1/2 inch depth.
Heat the oil over medium-high heat to 350 degrees F.
Fry the steak without crowding. If using a very small skillet, make sure the steak can lie flat in the oil.
Keep the oil between 325 degrees F and 350 degrees F while frying, adjusting the burner as needed after each steak goes in.
Cook each steak for 3 to 4 minutes on the first side, until the bottom is deep golden brown and crisp.
Flip with tongs and cook the second side for 2 to 4 minutes more, until deep golden brown and the center reaches at least 145 degrees F when checked through the side of the steak.
Use a fine-mesh skimmer or slotted spoon to remove loose fried crumbs if they start turning dark brown or black so the gravy does not taste bitter.
Transfer the fried steak to the clean wire rack and immediately sprinkle it with a tiny pinch of Diamond Crystal kosher salt.
Let the fried steak rest on the clean wire rack for at least 3 minutes before serving.
Carefully pour the hot oil into a heat-safe bowl or measuring cup, leaving the browned bits in the skillet.
Inspect the skillet before making gravy. Keep golden brown bits and toasted crumbs, but if any blackened flour crumbs smell burnt or harsh, carefully wipe those out with paper towels held in tongs.
Return 1 tablespoon of the frying oil to the skillet with the good browned bits and place the skillet over medium heat.
Add 1 tablespoon unsalted butter to the frying oil and let it melt.
Whisk the reserved 2 tablespoons clean all-purpose flour into the oil and butter until no dry flour remains.
Cook the flour and fat mixture for 2 to 3 minutes, whisking constantly, until it smells toasted and turns light tan.
Slowly whisk in 3/4 cup whole milk, adding a splash at a time and whisking smooth after each addition.

Whisk in 2 tablespoons heavy cream, 2 tablespoons low-sodium beef broth, 1/4 teaspoon Worcestershire sauce, the remaining 1/4 teaspoon garlic powder, the remaining 1/2 teaspoon black pepper, a small pinch ground white pepper, a small pinch celery salt, and 1/4 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt.
Simmer the gravy for 4 to 6 minutes, whisking often and scraping the browned bits from the bottom of the skillet, until thick enough to coat a spoon but still loose enough to pour.
If the gravy becomes thicker than you like, whisk in the remaining 1/4 cup whole milk a few tablespoons at a time until it is creamy and pourable.
Taste the gravy and adjust only if needed with a small pinch of kosher salt, black pepper, Worcestershire sauce, or hot sauce.
Spoon hot pepper gravy over the chicken fried steak right before serving so the crust stays crisp as long as possible.
Finish with 1 tablespoon finely chopped fresh parsley if using.
notes: Best meat choice: ask the butcher for top round cutlets tenderized once or twice, ideally about 1/3 inch thick and no thicker than 1/2 inch. That gives enough steak texture without making the crust over-brown before the meat is done.
Packaged cube steak is still the right classic option if it looks fresh, evenly tenderized, and about 1/3 to 1/2 inch thick. Avoid grey, wet-looking, ragged, paper-thin pieces.
Do not use cubed stew meat. Cube steak should look like thin steak with small tenderized marks across the surface, not chunks of beef.
If using Morton kosher salt instead of Diamond Crystal, use about two-thirds of the salt amounts listed in the instructions because Morton is denser by volume.
Do not extend the buttermilk soak past about 2 hours. The steak is already tenderized, and a very long acidic soak can push the texture in a softer, less steak-like direction.
Do not skip reserving clean flour for the gravy. Flour used to coat raw steak has touched raw beef and should not be used for quick gravy unless it is cooked thoroughly for food safety.

The biggest flavor moves are seasoning the meat before soaking, using hot sauce in the buttermilk, making clumps in the flour coating, coating the steak twice, frying on a rack instead of paper towels, and building the gravy from clean seasoned pan drippings.
The 15 minute coating rest matters because dry flour patches absorb moisture and the crust is less likely to slide off in the oil.
Peanut oil gives the cleanest classic fried flavor, but canola or vegetable oil still works well.
The oil temperature will drop when steak goes in. Start at 350 degrees F, then manage the burner so the oil stays mostly between 325 degrees F and 350 degrees F.
Skim loose crumbs if they start getting too dark. Burnt flour specks can make the gravy taste bitter.
A clean wire rack keeps the bottom crust crisp after frying. Paper towels trap steam and can soften the breading.
If you make a second steak, a 200 degrees F oven hold keeps the first steak hot without steaming the crust. Keep it uncovered on the rack and avoid holding longer than about 20 minutes.

Tenderized round steak and cube steak are lean and relatively thin, so the quality risk is overcooking after the crust is already brown. Pull the steak once the coating is golden brown and the center is at least 145 degrees F, then rest for at least 3 minutes.
Use the browned bits in the skillet for gravy only if they smell toasted and savory. If they smell burnt, remove the blackened crumbs and keep only the clean browned bits.
The beef broth, Worcestershire sauce, white pepper, and celery salt are small but important. They make the cream gravy taste savory instead of flat.
For a thicker diner-style gravy, simmer the gravy 1 to 2 minutes longer before adding any extra milk. For a looser gravy, use the reserved 1/2 cup whole milk.
Serve immediately with mashed potatoes, buttered corn, green beans, or a simple salad.
personalNotes: Good overall. The recipe has a lot of steps, but it is not hard if everything is measured and set up before cooking. Watch the oil temperature in different areas of the pan. The oil drops when the steak goes in, then can climb quickly if the pan stays on the burner. Adjust the burner or briefly move the pan off heat.
title: Chicken Fried Steak
totalTime: 1 hour plus 30 minutes to 2 hours buttermilk soak
